schools.summary: Matthew Henry sketches the whole reign in advance: Amaziah did right like Joash but not like David, beginning well yet not persevering.
Gill untangles the synchronism by a co-regency, dating the 'second year' to the second year of Joash of Israel's sole reign.
Clarke, following Calmet, reads the 'second year' as the first of Joash's independent government after a two-year co-regency with Jehoahaz.
Keil and Delitzsch weigh the reign: right in his father's spirit but 'not with a perfect heart' (2 Chron. 25:2), lapsing into idolatry at the last.
Gill measures the twenty-nine years, fourteen contemporary with Joash of Israel and fifteen after him.
Ellicott lets the Chronicler's 'not with a perfect heart' interpret 'not like David,' and Keil reads 'as Joash did' as the mark of an incomplete, divided piety.
Gill trims the praise: Amaziah did right 'externally, and at the beginning of his reign,' before the apostasy of its close.

themes: a right beginning is not a right heart

schools.summary: Ellicott renders the governing particle 'only' or 'save that' — the standing exception attached to nearly every commended king of Judah.
Gill traces the neglect back through the reigns: neither Amaziah nor his father Joash took away the high places.
Wesley gives the general law: 'it is hard to get clear of those corruptions which by long usage have gained prescription.'

themes: custom lends error a legal right

schools.summary: Gill explains the delay: the king waited until well settled, the old crimes forgotten, and the conspirators' friends dwindled away.
Clarke names the murderers Jozachar and Jehozabad and reads the pause as prudence, till the loyalty of other officers was assured.
Ellicott fixes 'confirmed in his hand' (cf. 1 Kings 2:46) and notes 'slew/slain' is literally 'smote/smitten.'
Clarke marks Amaziah's conscientious regard for the law of Moses; Wesley praises the faith and courage of obeying it though sparing the sons was hazardous.
Keil and Delitzsch set the mercy against Eastern custom, which would have slain the children too, and note the Chethib/Keri variant.
Gill reads the sparing of the children as clemency and strict regard for God's law, despite the risk of future revenge.

themes: the children of the murderers spared

schools.summary: Ellicott maps the Valley of Salt and identifies Selah with Petra; Keil stresses the enraged mercenaries who plundered Judah on their way home.
Clarke recounts the 300,000 troops and 100,000 dismissed Israelites, Selah as Petra; Wesley glosses Joktheel as 'the obedience of God.'
Gill tells the campaign: the hired troops dismissed at a prophet's word, ten thousand slain and ten thousand cast from the rock.

debates.question: What does the new name Joktheel mean?

sides.position: 'God's ward' (from its natural strength) or 'subjugated of God'
'the obedience of God,' given as a reward for the king's obedience to the prophet

themes: victory in the Valley of Salt
dismissing hired help at a prophet's word
the seeds of pride and the snare of Edom's gods

schools.summary: Keil finds the inward ground of the challenge in Amaziah's idolatrous pride; Ellicott judges he aimed at recovering the Ten Tribes for the house of David.
Clarke roots the war in the mercenaries' raids on Judah (2 Chron. 25:10ff.); Wesley in self-confidence and a desire of glory that he would first repent.
Gill combines the motives: revenge for the raids, an old grudge against Jehu's house, and a hope of reducing the ten tribes.
Matthew Henry brands the challenge proud, presumptuous, and prodigal of blood — 'a fool's lips enter into contention.'

debates.question: What moved Amaziah to challenge Jehoash?

sides.position: revenge for the outrages the dismissed Israelite mercenaries did to Judah
pride and ambition to recover the ten tribes for the house of David
both together — revenge and the hope of subduing Israel

themes: the Edomite gods and forgotten LORD

schools.summary: Keil reads the thorn-bush as a proud man overrating his strength and warns against over-reading; Ellicott ties the 'daughter' to Amaziah's demand and cites the versions and Jotham's fable.
Clarke decodes the figures (thistle=Amaziah, cedar=Jehoash, beast=his army); Wesley calls the thistle a low, troublesome shrub soon trodden down.
Gill notes the reply came back by the returning messengers.

themes: the parable of thistle and cedar

schools.summary: Ellicott unfolds the Hebrew — 'be honoured and abide at home,' 'why provoke disaster' — while Keil carries the story to the clash at Beth-shemesh.
Matthew Henry makes 'thine heart hath lifted thee up' the root of all sin, echoing Prov. 20:3 on the honour of ceasing from strife.
Clarke reads the defeat as God's judgment for the gods of Seir; Wesley urges contentment with glory already won.
Gill agrees pride lay at the bottom of the whole message, and goes before a fall.

themes: pride the root in the heart
sound counsel from an enemy's mouth
defeat as God's judgment for idolatry

schools.summary: Ellicott places Beth-shemesh at Ain-shems and reads Jehoash's approach to Jerusalem from the west (cf. 2 Kings 12:17); Keil sets it on the Judah-Dan border (Josh. 15:10).
Gill reads 'would not hear' as a judicial hardness of heart, permitted by God to punish the king's idolatry.

themes: the boast about to be answered

schools.summary: Ellicott explains the 'to his tents' variant as the disbanding of a beaten army (cf. 2 Kings 8:21); Keil carries the narrative on to the capture and the breached wall.
Gill, citing Josephus, says a sudden terror seized Judah so that they turned their backs before joining battle.
Wesley repeats the same tradition: they did not strike a stroke, but every man fled.
Ellicott details the captive king, the breach from the gate of Ephraim to the corner gate, and the insolence of the act; Keil confirms the return to Samaria with treasures and hostages.
Clarke notes Amaziah later regained his liberty on unrecorded terms; Wesley sees the abrupt pedigree as marking that he smarted for his ancestors' iniquity.
Gill catches the irony: now the kings 'looked one another in the face,' but Amaziah 'must look very silly.'
Ellicott hears poverty in 'that were found' and reads the hostages as Jehoash's calculated clemency; Keil explains why Jehoash's death-notice is repeated here from the Israelite annals.
Clarke traces 'hostages' to the root for a pledge (cf. Gen. 38:17), given by Amaziah's nobles or family to regain his liberty.
Gill notes the plundered vessels had been in the keeping of the family of Obededom.
Gill glosses 'his might' as Jehoash's valiant acts in the Syrian wars.
Clarke restricts 'how he fought with Amaziah' to the one battle already told, fuller in 2 Chron. 25:21ff.
Matthew Henry surveys three kings brought to their graves and reads the repeated formula as the historian gathering in the reigns.

Keil and Delitzsch use the death-notice as a hinge, freeing the historian to turn to Amaziah's later years and the conspiracy, whose cause they trace to his apostasy (2 Chron. 25:27), not the old defeat.
Gill glosses the idiom plainly: Jehoash 'died as they died.'

themes: the notice as a narrative hinge
the deferred cause of the conspiracy

schools.summary: Ellicott shows the figures self-consistent (2 Kings 13:10; 14:2) and infers Jehoash died soon after his victory.
Gill confirms the arithmetic and notes the Vulgate's variant 'twenty-five years.'
Matthew Henry draws the lesson: a man may live long after being shamed, thoroughly mortified yet not dead.
Gill distinguishes the lost royal annals here named from the canonical book of Chronicles.
Matthew Henry finds a verdict in the silence: Amaziah's acts are recorded but not his 'might,' for cruelty and bluster showed him void of true courage.

themes: cruelty and bluster are not valour

schools.summary: Keil dates the plot fifteen years after the defeat and grounds it in the king's apostasy (2 Chron. 25:27); Ellicott reads the anonymity as a general disaffection, popular or military, and notes Lachish a strong fortress.
Clarke traces the conspiracy to the lasting unpopularity of the defeat and its ruinous consequences.
Gill supposes the plotters were leading men of Jerusalem, aggrieved by the hostages and the sack of temple and city.
Matthew Henry: Amaziah was slain by subjects who hated him for his maladministration.

debates.question: What lay behind the conspiracy against Amaziah?

sides.position: the lasting unpopularity of his defeat by Jehoash and its consequences
his later apostasy, since the plot came fifteen years after the defeat
a broad, general disaffection at his misrule

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read the orderly conveyance, royal burial, and prompt succession of Azariah as proof the murder was a public deed of state, not private blood-revenge.
Gill records the rabbinic tradition that Amaziah was carried on horses, not men's shoulders, as a rebuke of his Edomite idolatry.

outliers.note: Preserves the rabbinic fancy that the king was borne on horseback (not on men's shoulders) because he had worshipped the horse-borne gods of Edom and neglected the God of Israel.

themes: burial in the city of David

schools.summary: Keil holds 'all the people' means the whole nation and the double name a genuine one; Ellicott reports Thenius's 'army' and infers from 'took' that Azariah was a younger son chosen as a favourite.
Clarke equates Azariah and Uzziah (2 Chron. 26:1); Wesley adds that his full reign began only twelve years later, the government meanwhile with protectors.
Gill notes the two names mean 'the help' and 'the strength' of the LORD.

debates.question: Does 'all the people of Judah' mean the whole nation or the army?

sides.position: the whole nation, in a national act of succession
the men of war / the army

themes: a younger son and a protectorate

schools.summary: Ellicott stresses the emphatic 'he' contrasting son with father and dates the deed after Amaziah's murder; Keil reads 'built' as fortified and sees Edom's subjugation completed.
Gill traces Elath's contested history from David through Edom's revolts to Amaziah's reconquest and his son's rebuilding.
Clarke locates Elath on the Elanitic Gulf, rebuilt by Uzziah and brought wholly under Judah.

themes: the vigour of the new king

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil judge the received 'forty-one years' irreconcilable with 2 Kings 15:8 and emend to fifty-one (Ewald, fifty-three), tracing the slip to confused Hebrew letters.
Matthew Henry warns against measuring character by length of reign or prosperity — one event befalls the righteous and the wicked.
Gill notes about fourteen or fifteen of Jeroboam's years were contemporary with Amaziah.

debates.question: How long did Jeroboam II really reign?

sides.position: fifty-one years; the received 'forty-one' is a confusion of Hebrew letters
holders: Thenius (as reported by Keil and Delitzsch)
Ewald (as reported by Keil and Delitzsch)

themes: the long reign of Jeroboam II

schools.summary: Matthew Henry presses that antiquity is no excuse: a sin is never the less evil for being an ancient usage.
Gill glosses the 'evil' as the persistent calf-idolatry of the ten tribes.
Keil and Delitzsch set the verdict of unbroken evil directly against the great restoration of the next verse, done 'according to the word of the LORD' by Jonah.

themes: an evil king and coming prosperity

schools.summary: Ellicott maps the frontier from Hamath to the Dead Sea and places Jonah's home Gath-hepher in Galilee (Josh. 19:13); Keil sees the borders of Solomon's day restored in the Pentateuch's own language.
Clarke measures the reconquest as restoring the kingdom to its extent at the disruption; Wesley identifies 'the sea' as the Dead Sea, the old plain of Gen. 13:10.
Matthew Henry marvels that God raised up the Galilean Jonah (cf. John 7:52) to promise favour and victory to Israel.
Gill reads it as the recovery of the border cities seized by Israel's enemies.

themes: from Hamath to the Dead Sea

schools.summary: Keil hears in 'none shut up nor left' the extremity of Deut. 32:36, met by God's compassion before the decree of rejection; Ellicott reads 'bitter' as 'stubborn,' inveterate (cf. 1 Kings 14:10).
Wesley says the bitterness moved God to help an unworthy people; Clarke describes an Israel so reduced that even captive and diseased felt God's heavy hand, till Jonah brought hope.
Matthew Henry dwells on God's 'bowels of mercy' (Jer. 31:20; Ps. 86:15), moved by affliction though no repentance is mentioned.
Gill reads the bitter affliction as the ground of God's compassion on a forlorn and helpless nation.
Ellicott explains 'blot out the name' as a figure from faded ink (Num. 5:23); Keil grounds the salvation by Jeroboam in the decree of rejection not yet gone out.
Matthew Henry reasons that because God had not said he would blot out Israel he would not do it — and the name endures in the gospel Israel (cf. 2 Kings 13:23).
Wesley: 'not yet; he had not yet declared this, as afterwards he did by the succeeding prophets.'
Gill notes the doom was afterward spoken by Hosea and fulfilled under Hoshea (2 Kings 17:6).

themes: the name not yet blotted out

schools.summary: Ellicott sets Jeroboam beside the Assyrian Ramman-nirari whose siege of Damascus opened the way, reading 'recovered' as 'restored'; over 'which belonged to Judah' he and Keil diverge.
Gill passes over the notice as the king's valiant and warlike exploits.
Clarke recalls David's conquest of Syria and garrisons in Damascus (1 Chron. 18:3ff.), now recovered by Jeroboam for Israel.

debates.question: How is 'Damascus and Hamath, which belonged to Judah, for Israel' to be construed?

sides.position: the parts of those kingdoms that had belonged to Judah under David and Solomon, now re-annexed to Israel
a restrictive epithet, 'Judaean Damascus and Hamath'; the text likely corrupt, with 'which belonged to Judah' to be cancelled

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch note the interregnum: Jeroboam died in Uzziah's twenty-seventh year, but Zachariah reigned only from the thirty-eighth (2 Kings 15:8).
Clarke traces the swift collapse into Zachariah, Shallum, and Menahem, and clears Amos of the slander at Bethel (Amos 7:10ff.); Wesley marks the rise of the writing prophets in this reign.
Gill notes only that the king died and was buried with his fathers.

themes: the coming anarchy of the north
the rise of the writing prophets
